title: Chapter 22: Vivienne's Choice
content: I was in two places at once, and both of them were killing me.

The cottage floor was ice beneath my knees — real ice, the kind that burned, the kind that meant the Hollow Council had pressed close overnight and left their frost on every surface. I could feel my body shivering in the present, could hear the low grinding of the wards as they held, barely, against the siege outside. The painting tugged at me from Julian's studio like a fishhook through the sternum. My edges were fraying. I could feel it — small unravelings at the borders of who I was, threads pulled loose by the portrait's insistence on reshaping me.
But at the same time, I was falling. Not downward. Inward. Through the golden thread that connected me to the sixth and final trapped spirit, the last Buena woman whose debt remained unpaid.
Vivienne.
The pull was enormous. She'd been waiting longest of all.
And so I split.

Part of me stayed in the cottage — hands braced against the frozen floorboards, breath clouding in the blue-white air, holding the line. And part of me fell through time, through the membrane between now and then, through the cold of the present into—
Warmth.

Sudden, disorienting warmth. Coal fire in a cast-iron grate. The smell of lavender and drying laundry. An apartment in 1932, small and tidy and frozen in the amber of a woman who had stopped the clock rather than let it finish.

I stood behind her, bodiless, watching. Vivienne Buena sat at a window that overlooked a train station. Her hands were folded in her lap. Her hair was dark and bound in a single thick braid that hung past her shoulders. She was not old — not yet — but her eyes had the glazed stillness of someone who had been sitting in the same position for a very long time.
Waiting.
"He might come back different," she said, without turning. She knew I was there. They always knew.

Her voice was calm. Practiced. I recognized the rhythm of it — the way you speak a sentence you've spoken so many times it no longer means anything, just a shape your mouth makes like prayer without faith.

"He might come back different," she said again. "Changed. Transformed. He was always interested in the new thinking. Perhaps he found something out there. Perhaps he's coming home on tomorrow's train."

Three years. Her husband had left on a train three years ago, objective time. But I could feel the distortion around her — the air thick and slow, humming with the particular resonance of a Buena curse bent inward. She'd been slowing time around herself. Stretching the wait. Making each hour last a day, each day a week, each month a season of its own.
Three objective years. Ten subjective years of waiting.

And the wait was the point. I saw it suddenly, the way you see the shape in a stereogram: the waiting wasn't hope. The waiting was armor. As long as she was waiting, she didn't have to arrive at the end. As long as tomorrow might bring him back, she never had to face the tomorrow where he didn't.

If she stopped waiting, she would have to grieve. And grief, for Vivienne, was a kind of death — the death of the woman who still believed he might walk through that door. She would rather be the woman waiting forever than the woman who'd been left.
Patience, weaponized against the self.

I had been in her position. Not literally — I'd never had a husband, never had a train to wait for — but I'd been her. I'd been waiting my whole life for the curse to break, for Blackwood Vale to release me, for some external force to arrive and change everything. Waiting felt like faith. But it wasn't. It was paralysis dressed in devotional clothing.

I could have comforted her. I could have sat beside her and said the things people say: *Maybe he will. Maybe tomorrow. Maybe you're right.* I could have let her wait another ten subjective years, another twenty, until her hair was white and her bones were dust and the train station outside had been demolished and rebuilt twice over.
But I hadn't come here to comfort her. I'd come here to free her.
"He won't," I said.
The words landed like stones in still water. Vivienne's hands tightened in her lap.
"He won't come back," I said. "He never does. You know."
Her shoulders drew up. A rigid, animal tension — the kind that precedes either flight or fight but precedes them by a long, trembling moment of held breath.
"You don't know that," she said. "You can't—"
"Vivienne."
She turned then. And I saw her face.
I had expected stubbornness. Defiance. The armor of denial that keeps a woman sitting at a window for ten years of her own making. But that's not what I saw. What I saw was worse.
She knew.

Her eyes were raw with knowing. Red-rimmed, exhausted, hollowed out by the very thing she refused to name. She'd known for years — perhaps from the beginning — that he wasn't coming back. The waiting wasn't ignorance. It was the alternative to feeling. She'd chosen limbo over hell because limbo was warm enough to survive and hell would burn her alive.
"I know," she whispered. The word cracked in her mouth. "I've always known."
"Then stop waiting."
Her face broke.

I don't mean she cried. I mean her face *broke* — like ice on a spring river, like a mask held together by hope that finally let go. Two centuries of careful composure, ten subjective years of trained patience, all of it fissuring at once. Lines appeared that hadn't been there. Her jaw trembled. And beneath the broken waiting face was another face — younger, rawer, terrified.

"If I stop waiting," she said, and her voice was the sound of something collapsing from the inside, "I have to feel it. All of it. Every year I pretended wasn't happening. Every season I told myself was just a long delay. I have to feel ten years of—"
"Yes."
"—of being left, of being—"
"Yes."
"—of being not enough to stay for—"

"Yes." I stepped closer. I was still half-elsewhere, still shivering in the cottage, still holding the wards, but here, now, in this warm apartment with its coal fire and its lavender and its frozen clock, I was close enough to see the wet shine in her eyes. "You have to feel it. And then it ends."
"It doesn't end," she said. "Grief doesn't end. It just—"

"It ends," I said. "Not the love. Not the loss. The waiting ends. The suspension ends. You feel it, and it burns through you, and then you are on the other side of it, and you are still alive, and the clock starts again."
She stared at me. Her hands were shaking.
"It's going to hurt," I said. "It's going to hurt more than anything you've ever felt. Because you've been holding it at bay for ten years and it's all going to hit you at once."
"I can't—"

"You can. Because you're a Buena woman, and our curses are our gifts in disguise. You've been using your Impatience to slow time, to stretch the wait, to make the agony last longer. But that's not what Impatience is for."
She looked at her hands. The shaking was worse now.

"Impatience is the refusal to wait," I said. "That's what it's always been. You turned it inward. You used it to make waiting eternal. But the real gift — the *true* gift — is the opposite. Impatience is the ability to make things end."
Vivienne closed her eyes.
And then she opened them, and something in her had shifted. Not broken — shifted. Like a gear finding its true alignment after years of grinding wrong.
"Do it," she said. "Make me feel it. All of it. Now."
She raised her hands. Not toward me — toward the clock on the mantle. Toward time itself.
And she used her curse the way it was meant to be used.

The air in the apartment *surged*. The slow, thick amber of suspended time shattered like glass, and in its place came a rush — a flood — a torrent of temporality so violent that the walls of the apartment seemed to bow outward. The coal fire flared white-hot and then orange and then red. The lavender smell sharpened to something almost chemical, then dissolved into ash. The light from the window flickered through a thousand sunrises and sunsets in the space of a breath.
Vivienne was accelerating time through herself.

The ten subjective years of waiting — ten years of frozen patience, of clock-stopped hope, of carefully maintained ignorance — collapsed. Not slowly. Not gradually. All at once. Three years of objective waiting and ten years of subjective denial compressed into a single moment of grief so immense that the room itself seemed to buckle under the weight of it.
She screamed.

It wasn't a human sound. It was the sound of a building collapsing in on itself — of load-bearing walls giving way, of floors pancaking downward, of a decade's worth of held grief finding its voice all at once. It came from somewhere deeper than her throat, somewhere in the marrow of her, and it was so loud and so full that I felt it in my chest like a physical blow, like a fist, like a demolition charge.

And the scream was *red*. I don't know how else to describe it. The grief was so dense it had color — a deep, arterial red that filled the apartment like smoke, that pressed against my skin with heat and weight, that tasted like copper and ash and the mineral tang of tears finally, finally being shed. Decades of withheld weeping, concentrated into one incandescent instant. It burned. It burned through her. It burned through the room. It burned through me, and I was only a witness.
I tasted it — the collapse of those years. Salt and soot and something older, like the mineral taste of stone after a century of rain. The flavor of time itself, finally allowed to pass.
And then—
Silence.
Not the silence of absence. The silence of aftermath. The silence that follows a fire, when the flames have eaten everything they can reach and what remains is honest and bare and still standing.

Vivienne stood at the window. The apartment around her was changed — the coal fire burned steadily, normally, without the amber glow of suspended time. The air was just air. The clock on the mantle ticked. Seconds passed. Real seconds.
Her husband was not on the platform below. No train was coming. The station was empty except for a woman with a pushcart and a boy chasing pigeons across the bricks.
And Vivienne looked at the empty platform, and she did not wait.
The waiting was over. Not because he came back. Because she stopped looking for him.

She turned to me, and she was transformed. The rawness was still there — it would be there for a while, I knew, perhaps forever in some small and livable way — but beneath it was something fierce and bright and young. She looked ten years younger. She looked like herself.
"The debt," I said.

"The debt is paid," she answered. And her voice was different — stronger, resonant, stripped of the careful placency of endless patience. "For the seventh. For the ending. For the women who need to stop waiting."
She stepped toward me, and light gathered around her — not the amber of frozen time but the sharp, clean gold of closure, of things finished, of doors finally shut.
"Don't you dare wait, Aurelia." Her eyes were fierce. Wet, but fierce. "Not even for a second. END things. It's the only mercy."

She pressed her hands to my chest, and the light poured into me — the gift of ending, of making things finish. The Impatience-Curse reforged not as the power to prolong suffering but as the power to burn through it, to compress agony into an endurable intensity, to choose the brutal mercy of conclusion over the slow death of hope.
And then the apartment dissolved, and the warmth with it, and I was back.
Back in the cottage. Back in the cold.

The frost had spread. It crept up the walls in crystalline fingers, thick along the windowsills, delicate and lethal. The Hollow Council had pressed harder while I was gone — I could feel the wards groaning, could feel the cold eating through the floorboards into my knees. My body ached from holding the line. My edges were more frayed than before; Julian's painting had been working while I worked, and I could feel the portrait's pull like a hook through the soft tissue of my identity, trying to reshape me into something more obedient, more still, more *painted*.
But in my chest, Vivienne's gift burned.
Not warmth. Something sharper. A furnace-core of refusal. The ability to end things.
I reached for my journal. My fingers were stiff with cold but I wrote anyway — the words coming fast and urgent, the Impatience of them no longer a curse but a discipline.
*Vivienne waited ten years in her mind for a man who was never coming back. I've been waiting my whole life for a curse to release me. But the curse won't release me. I have to release myself.*
I paused. The pen shook in my grip. The frost crept closer.
*No more waiting. No more patience.*
*It's time to END this.*

Five of six. Five debts paid. One spirit still bound. And me, Aurelia Buena, the last one standing between Blackwood Vale and whatever Julian was painting me into — no longer waiting for rescue, no longer hoping for a kinder ending, but choosing instead the brutal and beautiful mercy of conclusion.
The cottage shuddered. The wards flickered. Outside, the Hollow Council gathered themselves for another assault.
I closed the journal. I stood up.
And for the first time in my life, I did not wait for what came next.
